title: Plotting energyRt objects
text: example calendars dataset holds ready-made calendar objects. autoplot() (equivalent plot()) draws timeframe row rectangles sized slice’s share year:
code: data("calendars", package = "energyRt") names(calendars) #> [1] "season_dn"                      "d365"                           #> [3] "utopia_annual"                  "utopia_seasons"                 #> [5] "utopia_s4h24"                   "utopia_m12h24"                  #> [7] "d365_h24"                       "d365_h24_subset_1day_per_month" autoplot(calendars$season_dn)

title: Plotting energyRt objects
text: fill = "order" (default) color_pattern controls gradient. "within" (default) colours level slices — hourly row shows full h00→h23 cycle repeating every day, "global" colours absolute chronology across whole year.   fill metrics slice "share" "weight":
code: autoplot(calendars$d365_h24)                          # within (default) autoplot(calendars$d365_h24, color_pattern = "global") autoplot(calendars$season_dn, fill = "share")

title: Plotting energyRt objects
text: cell can labelled individual level name ("name", default, e.g. h00…h23) full slice path ("slice", e.g. d001_h00). Label text auto-contrasts fill; override label_color.
code: # zoom (see below) so labels are legible autoplot(calendars$d365_h24,          show_leafs = list(YDAY = "d001", HOUR = 1:24),          label_by = "name")

title: Plotting energyRt objects
text: show_leafs selects slices draw. Pass named list filter per timeframe level (character = slice names, numeric = positions among level’s slices), unnamed vector filter finest level directly.
code: # day 100, hours 5-10 autoplot(calendars$d365_h24, show_leafs = list(YDAY = "d100", HOUR = 5:10)) # the first 48 leaf slices (first two days) autoplot(calendars$d365_h24, show_leafs = 1:48)

title: Plotting energyRt objects
text: reduced calendar can shown full reference calendar: pass full one reference. slices present subset filled; rest left empty.
code: autoplot(calendars$d365_h24_subset_1day_per_month,          reference = calendars$d365_h24)

title: Plotting energyRt objects
text: border outlines rectangles (default dense rows read smooth gradient) palette picks viridis option; result ggplot can keep customizing :
code: autoplot(calendars$season_dn, palette = "magma", border = "grey40") +   labs(title = "Four seasons, day/night") +   theme_minimal()

title: Plotting energyRt objects
text: autoplot() lays every slice one row per timeframe. value indexed two-dimensional calendar — day--year × hour, month × hour — heatmap reads naturally: plot_heatmap() puts finest timeframe y, next x, coarser levels facets. layout taken calendar (pass calendar =), guessed slice names tsl_guess_format(). synthetic hourly load profile d365_h24 calendar (daily cycle plus seasonal swing). diurnal band winter/summer contrast immediately visible:  Facet day--year format "month" get one day--month × hour panel per month:  palette selects viridis option name labels colour bar, exactly autoplot().
code: cal  <- calendars$d365_h24 tt   <- cal@timetable prof <- data.frame(   slice = tt$slice,   load  = 50 + 30 * sin(2 * pi * (tsl2hour(tt$slice) - 6) / 24) +                 15 * cos(2 * pi *  tsl2yday(tt$slice)      / 365))  plot_heatmap(prof, calendar = cal, value = "load")   # x = YDAY, y = HOUR plot_heatmap(prof, value = "load", facet = "month")  # 12 monthly panels

title: Plotting energyRt objects
text: slice-indexed model data lays way. electricity demand solar capacity factor packaged UTOPIA kit, season × hour calendar (utopia_s4h24): pull object getObject(), take one region (, demand, one year), pass slice + value columns plot_heatmap(). name argument carries value’s unit onto colour bar.   weather heatmap exactly autoplot() draws weather object directly — line area variants (see Weather ).
code: repo <- utopia_modules$electricity$reg3$repo wcal <- calendars$utopia_s4h24  DEM <- getObject(repo, name = "DEM_ELC", drop = TRUE) dem <- subset(as.data.frame(DEM@dem), region == "R1" & year == 2050) plot_heatmap(dem[, c("slice", "dem")], calendar = wcal, value = "dem", name = "PJ") WSOL <- getObject(repo, name = "WSOL", drop = TRUE) wsol <- subset(as.data.frame(WSOL@weather), region == "R1") plot_heatmap(wsol[, c("slice", "wval")], calendar = wcal, value = "wval",              name = "capacity factor")

title: Plotting energyRt objects
text: horizon objects draw modelled intervals milestone years:
code: data("horizons", package = "energyRt") autoplot(horizons$Y2020_2060_by_5)

title: Plotting energyRt objects
text: commodity, autoplot() shows emission factors (@emis). Passing several commodities compares emission intensities side side.
code: COA <- newCommodity("COA", desc = "Coal",                     emis = data.frame(comm = "CO2", unit = "kt/GWh", emis = 0.33)) OIL <- newCommodity("OIL", emis = data.frame(comm = "CO2", unit = "kt/GWh", emis = 0.25)) GAS <- newCommodity("GAS", emis = data.frame(comm = "CO2", unit = "kt/GWh", emis = 0.18))  autoplot(COA)                 # a single commodity autoplot(COA, OIL, GAS)       # comparison

title: Plotting energyRt objects
text: process objects plotted year: points given data lines interpolation (extracted getData()). Level parameters faceted base name bounds prices/costs keep separate y-scales.  single given value (none) drawn flat dashed line showing interpolation direction. Pass years interpolate specific horizon:  Demand, import export follow pattern:
code: SUP_COA <- newSupply(   name = "SUP_COA", commodity = "COA", unit = "PJ", region = "R1",   availability = data.frame(     region = "R1", slice = "ANNUAL",     year   = c(2020, 2040, 2020, 2020),     ava.up = c(100,  200,  NA,   NA),     ava.lo = c(NA,   NA,   10,   NA),     cost   = c(NA,   NA,   NA,   30))) autoplot(SUP_COA) autoplot(SUP_COA, years = 2020:2050) DEM_ELC <- newDemand(   name = "DEM_ELC", commodity = "ELC", unit = "GWh",   dem = data.frame(region = "R1", slice = "ANNUAL",                    year = c(2020, 2030, 2050), dem = c(100, 150, 300))) autoplot(DEM_ELC) IMP_GAS <- newImport(   name = "IMP_GAS", commodity = "GAS",   imp = data.frame(region = "R1", slice = "ANNUAL",                    year = c(2020, 2050), imp.up = c(50, 80), price = c(5, 9))) autoplot(IMP_GAS)

title: Plotting energyRt objects
text: weather objects hold sub-annual factor — capacity / availability factor — per region slice. autoplot() offers three views via type =: calendar heatmap (default), diurnal line / area charts. slice layout (season × hour) can’t inferred slice names alone, pass model’s calendar; value’s unit (@unit, "capacity factor" unset) labels value axis — fill legend heatmap, y-axis line/area.  line view reads diurnal shape directly — one line per season, capacity factor y-axis:
code: WSOL <- getObject(utopia_modules$electricity$reg3$repo, name = "WSOL", drop = TRUE) wcal <- calendars$utopia_s4h24  autoplot(WSOL, calendar = wcal)                    # heatmap (default), faceted by region autoplot(WSOL, type = "line", calendar = wcal) autoplot(WSOL, type = "area", calendar = wcal)     # or filled areas

title: Plotting energyRt objects
text: Every method returns ggplot, + theme_*(), + labs(), + scale_*() etc. work. plot() available calendar horizon produces figure autoplot(). autoplot() also works result levcost() (levelized cost) — see ?levcost — requires solving small model, shown . See ?autoplot.calendar, ?autoplot.commodity, ?autoplot.supply, ?plot_weather full list arguments.

title: Solver backends and solver_options
text: See Installation Settings article install runtime library layer (en_check_dependencies(), en_install_deps()).

title: Solver backends and solver_options
text: solver_options named list ready-made presets — one pins backend, solver, data-exchange format, solver-specific tuning: Use preset one two ways: solve_mod() interpolates model solves ; solve_scen() solves already-interpolated scenario. solver = NULL scenario’s settings get_default_solver() used.
code: names(solver_options) solver_options$glpk solver_options$julia_highs_barrier # 1. Set it as the session default, then solve normally set_default_solver(solver_options$julia_highs) get_default_solver()                       # inspect the current default scen <- solve_mod(model)                   # uses the default  # 2. Pass it explicitly to a single solve scen <- solve_mod(model,  solver = solver_options$glpk) scen <- solve_scen(scen,  solver = solver_options$gams_gdx_cplex)

title: Solver backends and solver_options
text: GNU Linear Programming Kit. energyRt writes model GMPL/MathProg calls glpsol executable — lowest-friction option good default small medium models.
code: solve_mod(model, solver = solver_options$glpk)

title: Solver backends and solver_options
text: energyRt writes Pyomo ConcreteModel (data exchanged SQLite default, Arrow/feather *_arrow presets) solves configured solver. CBC open-source default; CPLEX GLPK also available.
code: solve_mod(model, solver = solver_options$pyomo_cbc) solve_mod(model, solver = solver_options$pyomo_cplex_barrier)   # CPLEX barrier

title: Solver backends and solver_options
text: energyRt writes JuMP model solves Julia. HiGHS (open-source) fast recommended choice large models; *_barrier, *_simplex *_parallel presets select HiGHS algorithm. Cbc, GLPK CPLEX also available.
code: solve_mod(model, solver = solver_options$julia_highs)           # default (auto) solve_mod(model, solver = solver_options$julia_highs_barrier)   # interior point solve_mod(model, solver = solver_options$julia_highs_parallel)  # parallel simplex

title: Solver backends and solver_options
text: GAMS license, energyRt can write run GAMS model. gams_gdx_* presets exchange data GDX (fast, native); gams_csv_* presets use text/CSV. *_barrier *_parallel presets inject tuned cplex.opt (LP method, threads, crossover, …).
code: solve_mod(model, solver = solver_options$gams_gdx_cplex) solve_mod(model, solver = solver_options$gams_gdx_cplex_barrier)

title: Solver backends and solver_options
text: NEOS Server runs commercial solvers (CPLEX, …) free internet. model built locally solve dispatched NEOS, can use CPLEX without local license. Two families provided — via Pyomo (neos_pyomo_*) via GAMS (neos_gams_*, sent inlined text local GAMS install needed). NEOS requires email address, set via NEOS_EMAIL environment variable helper: low-level NEOS interface (submit/poll/fetch job, list solvers, …) also exported — see ?neos neos_* functions.
code: set_neos_email("you@example.org") solve_mod(model, solver = solver_options$neos_gams_cplex) solve_mod(model, solver = solver_options$neos_pyomo_cplex_barrier)

title: Solver backends and solver_options
text: presets shipped package (generated installed data):

title: Solver backends and solver_options
text: preset plain list, can copy one tweak . Common fields:
code: # start from a preset and customise my_solver <- solver_options$julia_highs my_solver$name <- "julia_highs_custom" # ... adjust an inc* block to set HiGHS attributes ... solve_mod(model, solver = my_solver)

title: Solver backends and solver_options
text: Installation Settings — installing runtimes library layer. Model bricks — assembling commodities, processes structures model. ?solve_mod, ?solve_scen, ?set_default_solver, ?set_neos_email.

title: Introduction to energyRt
text: energy system network flows: primary resources extracted harvested, converted technologies, moved, stored, finally consumed useful energy. Planning system — build, , run — optimization problem: meet demand every place moment lowest total cost, subject physics policy. energyRt macro-language stating problem R. describe system — fuels, technologies, resources, demands — R objects, package compiles full capacity-expansion dispatch optimization model: derives sets, writes equations, runs solver, returns solution tidy tables. modeling layer (hundreds index-mapping bookkeeping decisions) generated, code stays level energy system .

title: Introduction to energyRt
text: energyRt optimization model — around one hundred predefined equations, extendable newConstraint() — implemented four mathematical programming languages: GLPK / MathProg — open source; bundled Rtools Windows, works box; Julia / JuMP — open source, fast solvers like HiGHS; Python / Pyomo — open source, CBC/HiGHS others; GAMS — commercial standard energy-systems work. model object solves backend consistent results — start GLPK zero setup, switch faster institutional solver later without touching model. full mathematical formulation documented model equations PDF; solver backends article covers configuration.

title: Introduction to energyRt
text: Models assembled small set object types: technology converts inputs outputs chain input → use → activity → output; costs, capacity, availability, emissions secondary flows attach . model bricks article walks every object depth.

title: Introduction to energyRt
text: One fuel, one power plant, one demand — complete, solvable system:  Bind bricks space time, solve GLPK: Results come back tidy tables: model built 1.59 GW gas capacity — exactly enough deliver 50 PJ year — solution reports every flow, cost, capacity variable level detail.
code: GAS <- newCommodity("GAS", timeframe = "ANNUAL") ELC <- newCommodity("ELC", timeframe = "ANNUAL")  SUP_GAS <- newSupply("SUP_GAS", commodity = "GAS",                      availability = data.frame(cost = 6.0))   # fuel price, MEUR/PJ  EGAS <- newTechnology("EGAS",   input   = list(comm = "GAS"), output = list(comm = "ELC"),   ceff    = data.frame(comm = "GAS", cinp2use = 0.55),        # 55% efficient   invcost = list(invcost = 900),                              # MEUR/GW   fixom   = 25, cap2act = 31.536, olife = 25L)  DEM_ELC <- newDemand("DEM_ELC", commodity = "ELC",                      dem = data.frame(dem = 50))               # 50 PJ a year  draw(EGAS) mod <- newModel("HELLO",   data    = newRepository("parts", GAS, ELC, SUP_GAS, EGAS, DEM_ELC),   region  = "R1", discount = 0.05,   horizon = newHorizon(period = 2025:2040, intervals = c(1, 5, 10),                        mid_is_end = TRUE))  scen <- solve_scenario(interpolate_model(mod, name = "BASE"),                        solver = solver_options$glpk,                        echo = FALSE)    # echo = FALSE silences the solver log getData(scen, "vObjective", merge = TRUE)   # total discounted system cost, MEUR #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   scenario name       value #>   <chr>    <chr>      <dbl> #> 1 BASE     vObjective 6677. getData(scen, "vTechCap",   merge = TRUE)   # capacity the model built, GW #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   scenario name     tech  region  year value #>   <chr>    <chr>    <chr> <chr>  <int> <dbl> #> 1 BASE     vTechCap EGAS  R1      2025  1.59 #> 2 BASE     vTechCap EGAS  R1      2030  1.59 #> 3 BASE     vTechCap EGAS  R1      2040  1.59

title: Introduction to energyRt
text: One language end end. Data preparation, model formulation, solving, analysis happen R: inputs arrive via tidyverse, results return tibbles ready dplyr ggplot2. export-import seam model analysis. Solver independence. Institutional modeling ecosystems typically bind one platform. energyRt’s four equivalent backends mean class can run GLPK, research group JuMP, agency GAMS — model, results. Built scale. sparse interpolation engine materializes set-tuples scenario needs, scenarios stored Arrow-backed folders (save_scenario() / load_scenario()) load lazily — larger--memory results remain workable. Analysis built . levcost() prices technology -priori (textbook LCOE unit-demand mini-model) ex-post solved scenario; report() renders technology process datasheet; draw() diagrams technology; autoplot() covers calendars, commodities, weather, costs . complete teaching model. UTOPIA electricity model ships package — step--step vignettes ready utopia_modules data kit scenario levers (CO2 cap, carbon tax, renewable share, nuclear moratorium) — path “hello world” policy analysis paved. energyRt sits alongside established energy-modeling frameworks (TIMES/MESSAGE/OSeMOSYS family) R-native alternative: class capacity-expansion models, formulated analyzed without leaving R.

title: Introduction to energyRt
text: Model bricks — every object type depth, including fuel blends, auxiliary flows, user constraints. vignette("utopia-build") — UTOPIA : build multi-region electricity model brick brick. vignette("utopia-use") — UTOPIA II: solve run policy scenarios. Solver backends — GLPK, JuMP, Pyomo, GAMS, NEOS. Workflow — extracting, editing, saving comparing scenarios.

title: Hello World!
text: energy system model optimizes technological chains meet given (exogenous) demand commodity (e.g. electricity, heat, etc.) using given resources (e.g. fuels, renewable energy sources, etc.) available technological options (e.g. power plants, heat pumps, etc.). minimal model must definition final demand available resources. Let’s start package settings continue minimal model definition. Check solver set (Optional) change default solver. Check/set directory store scenarios using set_scenarios_path get_scenarios_path functions. following minimal model consists commodity, supply, demand object. commodity object defined using newCommodity constructor-function. commodity object name description.  supply object defined using newSupply constructor-function. supply object name, description, commodity supplies, availability data. availability data data frame time-series costs prices commodity. simple supply object sets physical limits availability commodity. price set. Schematic representation supply object shows output arrow name supply object commodity supplies. inputs required “produce” commodity model. Demand opposite object supply. represents final consumption commodity model. minimal model can built now created objects. commodity supplied demanded. commodity price, demand exogenously set. optimization problem quite simple: minimize costs supplying demand. technological alternatives available yet model choose . constraints set resources. Let’s create model object solve . Several settings required parameterize model create model object: - region - name geographical region model defined. - data - repository object stores data objects used model. - calendar - calendar object defines time structure model. - horizon - horizon object defines time horizon model. - discount - discount rate used compute present value costs different time periods. repository object created combining data objects used model scenarios group scenarios. Additional data (model objects settings) can added particular scenario. calendar object defines time structure model: time-frames time-slices. Time-frames names time periods include time-slices. Time-slices minimal time units particular time-frame. Time-frames nested structure. top level year, name ANNUAL. default sub-anual resolutions set, simplest time structure.  Horizon object defines time horizon model. sequence milestone years periods milestone. example defines planning horizon 2025 2050 5-year intervals. One-year horizon: Multi-year horizon: newModel constructor-function combines configuration data objects create model object. model object created, can solved using solve function. solved (interpolated) model object becomes scenario object. Solving multiple years assumes perferct foresight given data future. Since haven’t defined years data, extrapolated default demand supply objects. tbc…
code: library(energyRt)  library(tidyverse) # for data manipulation and visualization my_solver <- get_default_solver() print(my_solver) set_default_solver(solver_options$julia_highs) get_scenarios_path() # define a commodity COM <- newCommodity("COM") # Declare a domestic supply (market) for the commodity SUP <- newSupply(   name = "SUP",    desc = "Domestic supply of commodity",   commodity = "COM",   availability = data.frame(     cost = 10 # costs or prices of the commodity     )   ) draw(SUP) DEM <- newDemand(   name = "DEM",   desc = "Final demand for commodity",   commodity = "COM",   dem = data.frame(     slice = "ANNUAL", # time-slices are defined in the calendar object     dem = 100     )   ) draw(DEM) # create a repository object to store the core model data world_repo <- newRepository(name = "world_repo", COM, SUP, DEM) # check content of the repository by type/class summary(world_repo) # check names of the repository objects names(world_repo) hello_calendar <- newCalendar() # default calendar with no sub-annual resolutions planning_2025 <- newHorizon(2025) planning_2025 planning_horizon <- newHorizon(   period = 2025:2050,    intervals = rep(5, 10)   ) planning_horizon mod <- newModel(   name = "MODEL",    region = "WORLD",   data = world_repo,   calendar = hello_calendar,   horizon = planning_2025,   discount = 0.05   ) class(mod) # solve for one year scen_2025 <- solve(mod, name = "HELLO_2025") summary(scen_2025) # solve for multi-year horizon by adding the horizon object scen_2050 <- solve(mod, name = "HELLO_WORLD",                     calendar = planning_horizon) summary(scen_2050)

title: Installation and Settings
text: Install R (≥ 4.3) — platform installers: Windows, macOS, Ubuntu/Debian. recommend RStudio IDE. quickest install one line — sets pak, reports Linux system libraries install, installs dependencies, energyRt : verify setup fresh session library(energyRt); en_setup(). Prefer install directly? energyRt’s CRAN dependencies pulled automatically pak (local sibling packages required): Installing energyRt install solver: still need least one backend (). GAMS GDX bridge (gdxtools) pulled later en_install_deps() use GAMS backend. Install fails Linux? install stops dependencies '...' available package 'energyRt', one CRAN import (often one needs system library) failed aborted rest. Install imports one one failure isolated reported: Linux, package fails compile missing system library. Ask pak apt packages required, install , re-run loop:
code: source("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/optimal2050/energyRt/master/inst/install.R") install_energyRt() install.packages("pak") pak::pkg_install("optimal2050/energyRt")  # or, with remotes: # install.packages("remotes") # remotes::install_github("optimal2050/energyRt") install.packages("pak")  # energyRt's direct CRAN imports (base packages are omitted): deps <- c(   "generics", "data.table", "DBI", "RSQLite", "tibble", "tidyr", "dplyr",   "rlang", "stringr", "lubridate", "purrr", "arrow", "progressr", "tictoc",   "cli", "zoo", "registry", "options", "glue", "plyr",   # suggested — plots and reports (optional but recommended, see section 7):   "ggplot2", "patchwork", "knitr", "rmarkdown", "tinytex", "sf" )  failed <- character(0) for (pkg in deps) {   ok <- tryCatch(     {       pak::pkg_install(pkg, ask = FALSE)       message("  [ok]   ", pkg)       TRUE     },     error = function(e) {       message("  [FAIL] ", pkg, ": ", conditionMessage(e))       FALSE     }   )   if (!ok) failed <- c(failed, pkg) }  if (length(failed) == 0) {   message("All dependencies installed — installing energyRt itself:")   pak::pkg_install("optimal2050/energyRt") } else {   message("Failed packages: ", paste(failed, collapse = ", ")) } pak::pkg_sysreqs("optimal2050/energyRt")   # lists the apt packages # e.g. sudo apt install libcurl4-openssl-dev libssl-dev libxml2-dev

title: Installation and Settings
text: en_check_dependencies() prints status table — backend: installed?, version, path, hint next — tells whether least one solver ready. backend also detector, e.g. en_check_julia(), en_check_python(), en_check_pyomo(), en_check_glpk(), en_check_gams(). en_check_packages() complements checking energyRt’s R dependencies, optional packages (plots/reports), external tools (LaTeX engine, MuseScore). en_setup() runs .
code: library(energyRt) en_check_dependencies()   # solver backends: GLPK / Julia / Python / GAMS / GDX en_check_packages()       # R packages, optional extras, LaTeX engine, MuseScore

what: 3. Install the system software (one or more backends)
title: Installation and Settings
text: Pick least one. manual steps; commands grouped operating system. Windows: already included — glpsol.exe ships Rtools 4.5 (already , since Rtools needed build energyRt source), C:/rtools45/x86_64-w64-mingw32.static.posix/bin. separate install. macOS: brew install glpk. Ubuntu/Debian: sudo apt install glpk-utils. glpsol detected, set path — set_glpk_path("C:/rtools45/x86_64-w64-mingw32.static.posix/bin") (macOS/Ubuntu package managers put PATH automatically). Windows: winget install julia -s msstore (official installer). macOS: brew install juliaup (curl -fsSL https://install.julialang.org | sh). Ubuntu: curl -fsSL https://install.julialang.org | sh. set_julia_path("~/.juliaup/bin/") Julia already PATH. Windows: run Miniforge3 installer; avoid Microsoft Store “App execution alias” python — en_check_python() flags non-functional. macOS: brew install miniforge (arm64/x86_64 installer). Ubuntu: bash Miniforge3-Linux-x86_64.sh releases. Windows: set_gams_path("C:/GAMS/<version>/"). macOS: set_gams_path("/Library/Frameworks/GAMS.framework/Versions/Current/Resources"). Ubuntu: set_gams_path("/opt/gams/gams<version>_linux_x64_64_sfx").

title: Installation and Settings
text: runtimes present, install packages backend needs one call: en_install_deps() runs dependency check, installs safe library layer runtimes finds (skipping — warning — runtime missing), re-checks end. can also install per backend:
code: en_install_deps()   # detects runtimes, then installs Julia + Python + R libs en_install_julia_pkgs()      # JuMP, HiGHS, Cbc, Clp, ... into the Julia env en_install_python_deps()     # conda env "energyRt" with pyomo + cbc (or pip) en_install_python_deps(use_conda = FALSE)  # pip into the configured python

title: Installation and Settings
text: helper fails, run steps hand. R layer — GAMS GDX bridge (CRAN) plus optional /O helpers: Julia layer — Julia REPL (julia -e "..."): Python/Pyomo layer — conda (recommended) pip:
code: pak::pkg_install("lolow/gdxtools")   # or remotes::install_github("lolow/gdxtools") install.packages(c("jsonlite", "readxl", "openxlsx")) using Pkg Pkg.add(["JuMP", "HiGHS", "Cbc", "Clp", "RData", "RCall",          "CodecBzip2", "Gadfly", "DataFrames", "CSV", "SQLite", "Dates"]) conda create -y -n energyRt python conda install -y -n energyRt -c conda-forge pyomo pandas pyarrow coincbc # pip fallback (no CBC binary): python -m pip install --upgrade pyomo pandas pyarrow

title: Installation and Settings
text: runtime PATH, set path (persisted energyRt options). Python/Pyomo, point conda environment created :
code: set_julia_path("~/.juliaup/bin/")             # same on all platforms set_python_path("~/.conda/envs/energyRt")     # or the env you created set_glpk_path("C:/rtools45/x86_64-w64-mingw32.static.posix/bin")  # Windows (Rtools 4.5); unneeded on macOS/Ubuntu set_gams_path("C:/GAMS/<version>/")           # see step 3 for macOS/Ubuntu paths

title: Installation and Settings
text: Re-check (everything installed now green), choose default solver, solve tiny model: two backends installed, solving scenario comparing objectives strongest check setup correct.
code: en_check_dependencies(solver_pkgs = TRUE)   # also verifies JuMP/HiGHS in Julia set_default_solver(solver_options$julia_highs_barrier)  # or solver_options$glpk  # a minimal model solve confirms the full toolchain works # (see the "Model bricks" article for assembling a model)

title: Installation and Settings
text: Everything optional — energyRt solves models without .
code: # plotting (all autoplot()/draw() charts) + levcost side-by-side panels install.packages(c("ggplot2", "patchwork"))  # report(): HTML datasheets & model/scenario reports need pandoc. # RStudio ships pandoc; outside RStudio install it from pandoc.org # (or reuse Quarto's: Sys.setenv(RSTUDIO_PANDOC = "<quarto>/bin/tools")). install.packages("rmarkdown")  # report(format = "pdf" / "tex") additionally needs a LaTeX toolchain: install.packages("tinytex") tinytex::install_tinytex()  # maps for plot_trade_map() / the utopia$map layouts install.packages("sf")  # GAMS gdx exchange # install.packages("gdxtools", repos = "...")   # see the gdxtools README

title: Model bricks
text: commodity quantity flows model. Energy carriers (COA, GAS, ELC, HEAT), environmental accounting commodities (CO2, NOx) materials (WATER, MAT) commodities — differ processes produce consume . fuel commodity can carry emission factors (emis): much environmental commodity released per unit burned. timeframe sets finest calendar level commodity tracked. commodity’s emission factors just data, can compared autoplot() (see autoplot article full set arguments):
code: COA <- newCommodity("COA", desc = "Coal", timeframe = "ANNUAL",                     emis = data.frame(comm = "CO2", unit = "kt/PJ", emis = 95)) GAS <- newCommodity("GAS", desc = "Natural gas", timeframe = "ANNUAL",                     emis = data.frame(comm = "CO2", unit = "kt/PJ", emis = 56)) ELC  <- newCommodity("ELC",  desc = "Electricity", timeframe = "HOUR") HEAT <- newCommodity("HEAT", desc = "Heat",        timeframe = "HOUR") CO2  <- newCommodity("CO2",  desc = "Carbon dioxide")   # accounting commodity names(getData(COA)) #> [1] "emis" autoplot(COA, GAS)   # emission intensity of the two fuels

title: Model bricks
text: Processes model elements move convert commodities. energyRt seven , every one draw() method renders schematic inputs, outputs, auxiliary commodities key coefficients.

title: Model bricks
text: source commodity, availability bound cost (see autoplot article -year view data).
code: SUP_COA <- newSupply(   name = "SUP_COA", desc = "Coal supply", commodity = "COA", unit = "PJ",   reserve = data.frame(region = "R1", res.up = 2e5),   availability = data.frame(region = "R1", year = NA_integer_, slice = "ANNUAL",                             ava.up = 1e3, cost = 10)) draw(SUP_COA)

text: commodity sink; dem demanded quantity years/slices.
code: DEM_ELC <- newDemand(   name = "DEM_ELC", desc = "Electricity demand", commodity = "ELC", unit = "GWh",   dem = data.frame(region = "R1", year = c(2020, 2050), slice = "ANNUAL",                    dem = c(100, 300))) draw(DEM_ELC)

title: Model bricks
text: Trade “rest world” price, bounded imp.* / exp.*.
code: IMP_GAS <- newImport(   name = "IMP_GAS", desc = "Gas import", commodity = "GAS", unit = "PJ",   imp = data.frame(region = "R1", year = c(2020, 2050), price = 6, imp.up = 500)) draw(IMP_GAS) EXP_OIL <- newExport(   name = "EXP_OIL", desc = "Oil export", commodity = "OIL", unit = "Mt",   exp = data.frame(region = "R1", year = c(2020, 2050), price = 500, exp.up = 100)) draw(EXP_OIL)

title: Model bricks
text: Moves commodity along routes (src → dst) regions transport efficiency teff. draw() shows flows one node time.
code: PIPE <- newTrade(   name = "PIPE", desc = "Gas pipeline", commodity = "GAS",   routes = data.frame(src = c("R1", "R2"), dst = c("R2", "R3")),   trade  = data.frame(src = c("R1", "R2"), dst = c("R2", "R3"), teff = c(0.97, 0.96))) draw(PIPE, node = "R2")   # imports from R1, exports to R3

title: Model bricks
text: Shifts commodity across time. seff holds charging/discharging/holding efficiencies (inpeff/outeff/stgeff) cap2stg storage duration.
code: STG_ELC <- newStorage(   name = "STG_ELC", desc = "Battery", commodity = "ELC",   seff = data.frame(inpeff = 0.9, outeff = 0.9, stgeff = 0.999),   cap2stg = 4,                                   # 4 hours of storage per unit power   aux  = data.frame(acomm = "MAT", unit = "kt"),   aeff = data.frame(acomm = "MAT", ncap2ainp = 0.25))  # material per new capacity draw(STG_ELC)

title: Model bricks
text: technology converts input commodities output commodities. Read diagram left--right four internal stages: cinp2use — much common use unit commodity input provides (e.g. converting fuels common energy basis). use2cact — use technology’s activity (central variable costs, availability capacity tied ). cact2cout — activity output commodity (efficiency / yield). three default 1.  four column headers box (inp, use, act, ) exactly stages; coefficient printed next flow scales.
code: input(s)  ──▶  use  ──▶  activity  ──▶  output(s)              cinp2use   use2cact      cact2cout BOILER <- newTechnology(   name = "BOILER", desc = "Gas boiler",   input  = data.frame(comm = "GAS",  unit = "PJ"),   output = data.frame(comm = "HEAT", unit = "PJ"),   ceff = data.frame(comm = c("GAS", "HEAT"),                     cinp2use  = c(1,  NA),                     cact2cout = c(NA, 0.9)),   # 90% efficiency   cap2act = 1) draw(BOILER)

title: Model bricks
text: several commodities interchangeable input (output) side, put group. group converted use (via ginp2use geff), member’s contribution bounded share (share.lo/share./share.fx). cinp2ginp converts commodity group’s common unit.  share range drawn square brackets next grouped commodity. Mixed units live coefficients. inputs outputs use different units, unit conversion must ride one chain coefficients use, activity cap2act agree. fuels PJ electricity GWh: ginp2use = 277.78 converts fuel group GWh (technology operates GWh), cap2act = 8760 matches (1 GW × 8760 h). Keeping every commodity one unit family (UTOPIA PJ) avoids gymnastics — convert("PJ", "GWh", 1) tells factor can’t.
code: CHP <- newTechnology(   name = "CHP", desc = "Co-firing plant (coal + biomass)",   input = data.frame(comm = c("COA", "BIO"), group = "fuel", unit = "PJ"),   output = data.frame(comm = "ELC", unit = "GWh"),   group = data.frame(group = "fuel", desc = "Blended fuel", unit = "PJ"),   # inputs are PJ but the output is GWh: the PJ->GWh conversion (1 PJ =   # 277.78 GWh) rides on `ginp2use`, so use/activity are measured in GWh   geff  = data.frame(group = "fuel", ginp2use = 277.78),   ceff  = data.frame(comm = c("COA", "BIO", "ELC"),                     cinp2ginp = c(1, 1, NA),                     cact2cout = c(NA, NA, 0.4),     # 40% efficiency                     share.up  = c(1.0, 0.3, NA)),   # at most 30% biomass   cap2act = 8760)                                   # 1 GW x 8760 h = 8760 GWh draw(CHP)

title: Model bricks
text: Everything technology measured activity. Installed capacity limits maximum activity scalar cap2act: cap2act — “much product (activity, output commodity identical) produced per unit capacity”. power plant capacity GW, cap2act = 8.76 gives maximum activity 8.76 GWh per GW per year (8760 h, scaled cost/energy units use). Capacity bounded capacity slot: stock (pre-existing), cap.lo//fx (total), ncap.lo//fx (new builds) ret.lo//fx (retirement). Availability factors af/afs bound activity within capacity.

previous_headings: Processes > Anatomy of a technology > Activity, capacity and units
what: Capacity in input vs. output units
title: Model bricks
text: capacity tied activity, whether expressed input output units depends place efficiency. Keep cinp2use = 1 put loss output (cact2cout = 0.9) activity tracks input — capacity fuel-input units. Move efficiency input side instead capacity becomes output rating:  boilers 90% efficiency; differ unit capacity means (fuel input vs. heat output).
code: # capacity rated on OUTPUT (e.g. a 1 GW_e turbine): activity == output BOILER_out <- newTechnology(   name = "BOILER_out", desc = "Boiler rated by heat output",   input  = data.frame(comm = "GAS",  unit = "PJ"),   output = data.frame(comm = "HEAT", unit = "PJ"),   ceff = data.frame(comm = c("GAS", "HEAT"),                     cinp2use  = c(1 / 0.9, NA),   # efficiency on the input side                     cact2cout = c(NA, 1)),        # activity == heat output   cap2act = 1) draw(BOILER_out)

title: Model bricks
text: Auxiliary commodities extra flows tracked alongside main conversion — emissions (NOx, SO2, CH4, PM10, PM25), recovered HEAT, land, cooling WATER, construction MATerials, -products. declared aux linked aeff coefficient named <driver>2a<|inp>: driver scales flow: cinp (commodity input), cout (output), act (activity), cap (installed capacity), ncap (new capacity), storage terms; …2aout produces aux commodity (emissions, recovered heat, -products); …2ainp consumes (water, materials, energy). tab isolates one driver base technology (GAS → ELC).
code: aux_tech <- function(param, value, acomm = "AUX", unit = "unit") {   aeff <- data.frame(acomm = acomm, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)   aeff[[param]] <- value   newTechnology(     name = paste0("TECH_", param), desc = paste0("aux via ", param),     input  = data.frame(comm = "GAS", unit = "PJ"),     output = data.frame(comm = "ELC", unit = "GWh"),     ceff   = data.frame(comm = c("GAS", "ELC"), cinp2use = c(1, NA), cact2cout = c(NA, 0.4)),     aux    = data.frame(acomm = acomm, unit = unit),     aeff   = aeff) }

title: Model bricks
text: Real technologies carry several aux flows . coal plant might emit NOx, SO2, CH4, PM10 PM25, recover HEAT -product, consume cooling WATER construction MATerials — single aux/aeff pair, row filling driver column applies:
code: PLANT <- newTechnology(   name = "PLANT", desc = "Coal plant with a full aux inventory",   input  = data.frame(comm = "COA", unit = "PJ"),   output = data.frame(comm = "ELC", unit = "GWh"),   ceff = data.frame(comm = c("COA", "ELC"), cinp2use = c(1, NA), cact2cout = c(NA, 0.4)),   aux  = data.frame(     acomm = c("NOx", "SO2", "CH4", "PM10", "PM25", "HEAT", "WATER", "MAT"),     unit  = c("kt",  "kt",  "kt",  "kt",   "kt",   "PJ",   "Mm3",   "kt")),   aeff = data.frame(     acomm     = c("NOx", "SO2", "CH4",  "PM10", "PM25", "HEAT", "WATER", "MAT"),     act2aout  = c(0.20,  0.01,  0.001,  0.05,   0.03,   0.9,    NA,      NA),     act2ainp  = c(NA,    NA,    NA,     NA,     NA,     NA,     0.1,     NA),     ncap2ainp = c(NA,    NA,    NA,     NA,     NA,     NA,     NA,      2))) draw(PLANT)

title: Model bricks
text: technology operates timeframe — level calendar dispatched . default finest (highest-frequency) timeframe among commodities uses: plant producing hourly ELC runs hourly, one producing annual STEEL runs annually. Set timeframe = force coarser level (e.g. run electricity plant "SEASON" rather "HOUR" shrink model).
code: newTechnology(   name = "WIND", input = data.frame(comm = character()),   output = data.frame(comm = "ELC", unit = "GWh"),   timeframe = "HOUR")            # dispatch hourly (else inferred from commodities)

title: Model bricks
text: Beyond physics baked process, can add linear constraints model variables. newConstraint() low-level form: name raw model variable, dimension (.), relation (eq) right-hand side (rhs). classic use economy-wide emission cap: newConstraintS() higher-level shortcut: instead naming variables pass semantic type ("capacity", "newcapacity", "inp", "", "share", "growth", …) subset processes, fills right variables — instance capping total new capacity technology:
code: CO2CAP <- newConstraint(   name = "CO2CAP", desc = "Economy-wide CO2 cap", eq = "<=",   for.each  = data.frame(year = c(2030, 2040, 2050), comm = "CO2"),   emissions = list(variable = "vEmsFuelTot"),         # LHS term   rhs       = data.frame(year = c(2030, 2050), rhs = c(5000, 2000)),   defVal    = Inf) CO2CAP@rhs        # the emission-cap path (interpolated over the horizon) #>   year  rhs #> 1 2030 5000 #> 2 2050 2000 # limit total new coal capacity across the horizon COALLIM <- newConstraintS(   name = "COALLIM", type = "newcapacity", eq = "<=",   for.sum = list(tech = "PLANT"), rhs = 50)

title: Model bricks
text: bricks collected three nested containers.

title: Model bricks
text: repository unordered bag commodities processes — reusable “parts library” draw model . Pass objects straight newRepository(), add() later. Members reachable name (repo$BOILER, repo[["COA"]]), length() / print() summarise bag.
code: repo <- newRepository("demo", COA, GAS, ELC, HEAT, CO2,                       SUP_COA, DEM_ELC, IMP_GAS, BOILER) repo <- add(repo, PLANT)          # add more parts later names(repo) #>  [1] "COA"     "GAS"     "ELC"     "HEAT"    "CO2"     "SUP_COA" "DEM_ELC" #>  [8] "IMP_GAS" "BOILER"  "PLANT"

title: Model bricks
text: model binds repository dimensions solved : region set, calendar (sub-annual structure — see time-resolution article) horizon (milestone years). See autoplot article plots calendar horizon bricks.
code: mod <- newModel(   name     = "demo",   data     = repo,   region   = "R1",   calendar = calendars$season_dn,   horizon  = newHorizon(period = 2020:2050, intervals = c(1, 10, 10, 10))) mod #> Name:  demo

title: Model bricks
text: scenario model prepared specific solver run — parameters interpolated onto horizon, settings attached, (solving) results stored. interpolate_model() builds one without touching solver; actual optimisation solve_scenario(), covered solver backends article.
code: scen <- interpolate_model(mod, name = "BASE")   # no solver needed # scen <- solve_scenario(scen, solver = "GLPK") # the optimisation step

title: Model bricks
text: technology sits container can price output levcost(). Given repository model technology name, builds solves tiny unit-demand model around technology, drawing related commodities supplies – , model, region / calendar / discount – container. solved scenario instead reports realized (ex-post) cost read solution. default levcost() prices annual timeframe (weather profile collapses annual capacity factor, sizing capacity serve demand factor); pass timeframe = "native" keep model’s sub-annual calendar normalise total generation. input commodity supply container returns NULL message unless autocomplete = TRUE (fuel_costs = price) supplies . See UTOPIA (-priori screening across technologies) UTOPIA II (ex-post cost autoplot) worked numbers.
code: levcost(mod,  name = "PLANT")                        # a-priori LCOE from the model levcost(mod,  name = "PLANT", autocomplete = TRUE)   # if an input lacks a supply levcost(scen, name = "PLANT")                        # ex-post cost (solved scenario) report(scen,  name = "PLANT", format = "html")       # datasheet with cost embedded
